source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Most ASICs other than Trainium and TPU will fail; only those with multi-generation experience will be competitive
summary_extended: Baker argues that the ASIC market for AI accelerators will consolidate to only two viable players — Google's TPU and Amazon's Trainium — because building a competitive ASIC requires at least three chip generations of accumulated learning, a bar that almost no other company can meet. He supports this with the historical example of Google's TPU, which was not competitive until v3/v4, and notes that Amazon's Trainium 3 is the first generation that is merely 'okay,' with Trainium 4 likely to be genuinely good. Baker explicitly states he will be surprised if any ASICs beyond Trainium and TPU succeed, implying that all other ASIC efforts — from startups and hyperscalers alike — will fail to achieve competitive scale. Compounding this, Nvidia's annual GPU cadence raises the full-stack competitive bar continuously, making it structurally harder for any new ASIC entrant to catch up. The investment implication is that Nvidia remains the dominant AI accelerator beneficiary by default, while Amazon and Google are the only credible internal ASIC challengers, and AMD and other GPU/ASIC competitors are unlikely to displace this dynamic.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Anthropic is burning dramatically less cash than OpenAI while growing faster, benefiting from Google/Amazon TPU and Trainium access
summary_extended: Baker argues that Anthropic is demonstrating superior capital efficiency relative to OpenAI, burning dramatically less cash while growing faster, which he attributes in large part to its privileged access to Google TPUs and Amazon Trainium chips — compute infrastructure that carries lower cost than Nvidia GPUs. He notes that Anthropic's cloud partnerships with Google and Amazon effectively give it the same low-cost compute advantages that Google enjoys internally, structurally advantaging it versus OpenAI which relies more heavily on expensive Nvidia hardware. Baker views Anthropic's recent $5B deal with Nvidia as strategically significant, interpreting it as a signal that CEO Dario Amodei believes Blackwell and future Rubin GPUs will be superior to TPUs and that Anthropic is hedging its compute stack accordingly. The investment implication is not a direct public equity play on Anthropic itself (private), but Baker's framing implicitly credits Google and Amazon as infrastructure beneficiaries whose TPU and Trainium platforms are enabling a credible, capital-efficient challenger to OpenAI.

source: Heller House / Mission Control — SpaceX IPO Week — CFO Bret Johnson Profile
summary: SpaceX's xAI acquisition is generating massive compute rental revenue ($1.25B/month from Anthropic, $920M/month from Google) but this is datacenter rental revenue, not AI product revenue
summary_extended: Baker (or the reporter profiling SpaceX CFO Bret Johnson) argues that SpaceX/xAI's reported AI revenue is a quality-of-earnings concern because it is derived from compute rental deals — specifically $1.25B/month from Anthropic and $920M/month from Google — rather than organic AI product demand. The thesis holds that xAI overbuilt datacenter capacity and, following cost cuts and researcher departures post-acquisition, is monetizing excess capacity by renting it to third parties. This is structurally different from the subscription and API revenue that OpenAI or Anthropic generate, making SpaceX's AI revenue growth optically impressive but fundamentally lower quality. The investment implication is a cautionary flag for any SpaceX IPO valuation that ascribes high multiples to AI revenue, as that revenue is capacity utilization income contingent on third-party demand rather than proprietary product traction. No specific timeframe is given, but the concern is framed as near-term and directly relevant to SpaceX's IPO narrative.

source: HedgeFundAlpha (writeup) — Baker & Khaira fireside writeup
summary: Memory cycle dynamics represent the most important investment judgment call Baker has faced in his career
summary_extended: Baker explicitly described the memory cycle thesis as the most important investment judgment call of his entire 25+ year career as a technology investor, signaling extraordinarily high conviction that a major inflection point in DRAM and HBM supply-demand dynamics is imminent or underway. While the public writeup does not disclose his directional view (bullish or bearish), the framing implies he sees a structural shift — likely related to the intersection of AI datacenter HBM demand and traditional DRAM cycle dynamics — that is not yet fully priced into markets. The investment implication is that memory-exposed equities, particularly those with significant HBM or DRAM exposure, represent a high-stakes positioning opportunity. Baker's career context as a long-tenured technology investor makes this framing unusually significant, as such superlatives are rarely deployed and suggest he believes consensus is materially wrong on the direction or magnitude of the memory cycle turn.

source: Sohn NY 2026 — Tech investing fireside
summary: Neo/specialist clouds (CoreWeave, Crusoe, Nebius, Lambda) represent a durable business model, not a transitional arbitrage
summary_extended: Baker argues that neo/specialist cloud providers — companies like CoreWeave, Crusoe, Nebius, and Lambda — represent a structurally durable business model rather than a transitional arbitrage that will eventually be displaced by hyperscalers. His core reasoning is that running GPU clusters at high utilization is operationally extremely difficult, analogous to piloting a Formula 1 car, and that CoreWeave in particular achieves 2–3x higher GPU utilization per hour than lower-quality competitors, constituting a genuine and defensible operational moat. He contends that hyperscalers were historically locked in a cost-minimization mentality and are only now beginning the cultural shift required to compete effectively in this space, meaning the window for specialist operators remains open. Atreides holds a disclosed large position in Crusoe, and Baker notes he was conflicted out of CoreWeave at a $1.1B valuation, underscoring his long-term conviction in the category.

source: Sohn NY 2026 — Tech investing fireside
summary: Terrestrial data center power, cooling, and industrial infrastructure names face structural short risk as orbital compute approaches
summary_extended: Baker argues that terrestrial data center infrastructure companies — specifically those in power delivery, cooling systems, and industrial build-out — face a structural short risk as orbital compute matures over the next 5–7 years. His reasoning is that space-based compute eliminates the two largest cost and infrastructure inputs of terrestrial data centers: power (provided by the sun in orbit) and cooling (provided by the cold dark side of a satellite), making the entire terrestrial infrastructure stack potentially obsolete at scale. Companies that have massively expanded capacity to serve the current AI-driven data center boom will face a 'screeching halt' in demand as orbital compute takes share, and the transition period itself will be 'very painful' for these names. The investment implication is a medium-to-long-term structural short on power, cooling, and industrial infrastructure companies levered to terrestrial data center construction, though Baker acknowledges the 5–7 year timeline means this is not an imminent catalyst. No specific tickers were named, and the implied short candidates — while conceptually clear as a category — are too numerous and ambiguous to identify unambiguously from the thesis alone.

source: Sohn NY 2026 — Tech investing fireside
summary: TSMC's deliberate capacity restraint will prevent an AI bubble and sustain a prolonged upcycle in leading-edge wafer supply
summary_extended: Baker argues that TSMC is deliberately constraining leading-edge wafer capacity expansion far below the pace that Nvidia's Jensen Huang demands, and that this intentional restraint is the key structural difference between the current AI upcycle and prior technology bubbles. He characterizes TSMC's leadership as stewards of Morris Chang's legacy — 'flinty old men and women' — who expand capacity at roughly 5% when Huang visits every three months requesting doublings or triplings. Because physical wafer supply cannot be conjured quickly, Baker contends this bottleneck acts as a natural governor on speculative overinvestment, preventing the kind of bubble that unconstrained capital allocation would otherwise produce. The investment implication is that the AI infrastructure upcycle will be prolonged and structurally sound rather than bubble-prone, and that TSMC as the sole supplier of leading-edge capacity is the critical chokepoint — and thus a core long. Baker notes that Nvidia alone could sell $1–2 trillion of chips if TSMC capacity were available, underscoring the magnitude of suppressed demand flowing through TSM's fabs.

source: HedgeFundAlpha (writeup) — Baker & Khaira fireside writeup
summary: Amazon's Trainium chips are the most underestimated AI play in the market
summary_extended: Baker argues that Amazon's Trainium custom AI training silicon represents the most underestimated AI investment opportunity in the market, suggesting the street is systematically mispricing this capability within AMZN shares. His core reasoning is that Trainium reduces AWS's dependence on Nvidia GPUs, which carries significant margin and competitive implications: as AWS deploys more proprietary silicon, it captures more of the value chain internally rather than paying Nvidia's premium pricing. The investment implication is that AWS margin expansion and competitive positioning in AI cloud services are not yet reflected in AMZN's valuation, making it a contrarian long. Baker's use of superlative framing ('most underestimated AI play in the market') signals this is a prepared, high-conviction differentiated view rather than a passing observation. No specific revenue figures or timeframes were cited, but the medium-term horizon implies Baker expects Trainium's impact to become visible in AWS financials within the next one to three years.

source: Sohn NY 2026 — Tech investing fireside
summary: AI frontier model pricing is shifting from flat-rate subscriptions to usage-based with overage, which is wildly bullish for AI revenue and token pricing
summary_extended: Baker argues that the AI frontier model industry is undergoing a fundamental pricing shift analogous to the cellular industry's transition from flat-rate minute plans to per-minute overage pricing — a shift he characterizes as 'wildly bullish' for AI revenue and token economics. He contends that usage-based pricing with overages, now being adopted by leading frontier model providers, will dramatically expand monetization per user and unlock massive revenue growth. A key structural observation is that only 10 basis points (0.1%) of the global population currently uses frontier AI models at the intensity they should, and scaling that to even 5% of global population would create compute demand that is, in his words, 'unimaginable.' Baker endorses a near-term combined revenue target of $200B+ for OpenAI and Anthropic, implying these two private companies alone could rival the largest software businesses ever built. The investment implication is bullish on the entire AI compute and infrastructure stack, but the direct revenue beneficiaries — OpenAI and Anthropic — are private and not US-listed equities, leaving no directly actionable public ticker from this specific thesis.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: AI has net destroyed trillions of dollars of value at the application layer, and value creation is concentrated in companies with the highest ratio of utilized GPUs per human
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI has been a net destroyer of value at the application layer, having wiped out trillions of dollars in market capitalization even when accounting for the success stories like Cursor and Cognition. His reasoning is structural: the companies that are actually creating economic value in the AI era are those with the highest ratio of utilized GPUs per human employee, meaning capital-efficient, AI-native businesses rather than traditional software companies layering AI onto legacy architectures. The implication is deeply bearish for incumbent SaaS companies, which face disruption from leaner AI-native competitors that can deliver similar or superior functionality with far fewer human resources. Baker frames this as an empirical observation investors must internalize, not a speculative view — the value destruction has already occurred and the structural dynamic is already in motion. Investors should be cautious of legacy application-layer software companies and skeptical of the assumption that AI is uniformly additive to enterprise software valuations.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Astera Labs is a high-conviction long that has been miscategorized as a 'copper loser' in market baskets, when it is structurally a beneficiary of both copper and optical connectivity
summary_extended: Baker argues that Astera Labs (ALAB) has been systematically miscategorized in market 'copper loser' baskets, creating a significant mispricing opportunity. His structural argument is that Astera's primary product is a switch, and switches by definition connect to accelerators using both copper and optical connectivity — meaning Astera cannot be a copper loser regardless of how the market has labeled it. Baker has high conviction rooted in a long relationship with the company dating back to a Series C investment, giving him deep fundamental knowledge of the business. The miscategorization in leveraged short baskets has artificially depressed the stock, and Baker frames this as one of the largest opportunities he sees, contrasting ALAB with low-quality names that have already run 10x in 3-6 months and are likely to revert. The investment implication is a long position in ALAB, which benefits structurally from both copper and optical connectivity adoption in AI datacenter infrastructure.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Google has lost its per-cost-token leadership due to conservative TPU v8 design decisions, shifting the Pareto frontier to Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI
summary_extended: Baker argues that Google has lost its dominant position on the AI Pareto frontier (intelligence vs. cost-per-token) over the past nine months, with Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI's Grok now leading while Google's Gemini 3.1 merely 'hangs on' to the frontier — and likely only through subsidization driven by competitive pride. The root cause, Baker contends, was a deliberate and conservative design decision on TPU v8 intended to reduce Google's dependence on Broadcom (for networking) and Nvidia (for GPUs), which resulted in a chip that underperforms on the cost-efficiency curve relative to competitors using more aggressive third-party silicon. The investment implication is negative for Google's AI monetization and cloud competitiveness, as losing cost-per-token leadership means Google cannot attract or retain the most cost-sensitive AI workloads. Baker frames this as a structural, self-inflicted competitive disadvantage rather than a temporary gap, suggesting the shift in frontier leadership may persist until TPU v9 or a course correction in chip design philosophy. All three frontier leaders — Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI — remain private, limiting direct investable expression of the beneficiary side of this thesis.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Microsoft flinched on AI capex in early 2025 and is now making the right but risky decision to use compute for internal products rather than selling to OpenAI — this is underappreciated by the market
summary_extended: Baker argues that Microsoft made a critical strategic error in early 2025 by briefly pulling back on AI capex, causing it to lose compute allocations that are difficult to recover — a mistake he summarizes as 'if you flinch, you lose position.' Satya Nadella is now pivoting to use that compute internally to improve Copilot and train Microsoft's own frontier models rather than selling capacity to OpenAI, which Baker views as the right long-term strategic bet but a risky one given his skepticism about Microsoft's ability to compete at the frontier model level. Baker provides a specific counterfactual: if Microsoft had simply continued serving OpenAI and Anthropic with its GPU capacity as a pure infrastructure play, the stock would be worth $800 today, implying the market is not fully pricing in the execution risk of the internal AI product strategy. The investment implication is nuanced — Baker gives Satya credit for strategic courage but is not unambiguously bullish, flagging team capability as the key risk to watch on the internal model training ambition.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Companies in the power production and cooling ecosystem that are massively ramping capacity face risk from orbital compute becoming real just as that capacity hits
summary_extended: Baker warns that companies in the terrestrial power production and cooling ecosystem that are currently ramping significant new capacity face a specific timing risk: orbital compute could become understood and priced by the market precisely as that new capacity comes online, potentially stranding or devaluing those investments. His reasoning is that if orbital compute eliminates or dramatically reduces the need for earth-based data center power and cooling infrastructure, the massive capital expenditures being made today by power and cooling companies could become impaired assets. The investment implication is a cautionary one — Baker is not explicitly shorting these names but urges investors in power/cooling infrastructure plays to think carefully about whether the timeline of their capacity additions aligns dangerously with the timeline of orbital compute becoming a credible market narrative. He stops short of naming specific companies, framing this as a structural risk warning rather than a specific sell recommendation, and the thesis is explicitly contrarian relative to the consensus bullish view on AI power infrastructure buildout.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: TSMC's capacity discipline is the single most important variable in preventing an AI infrastructure bubble
summary_extended: Baker argues that TSMC's capacity discipline is the single most important variable determining whether AI infrastructure investment tips into an overbuild bubble. His core claim is that if TSMC were to fulfill Jensen Huang's full demand ambitions, Nvidia could theoretically sell $2–3 trillion worth of GPUs in 2026–27, a pace that would almost certainly result in massive overcapacity and a bubble. Baker identifies a 'Goldilocks zone' for TSMC: expand capacity enough to prevent Intel or Samsung from capturing more than roughly 30% market share as a second-source foundry (which would undermine TSMC's pricing discipline), but constrain supply enough to prevent runaway GPU shipments. He concludes that TSMC's capacity announcements and expansion decisions are the single best leading indicator an investor can monitor to assess AI infrastructure bubble risk, making TSM the central analytical lens for this thesis.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Amazon is in a strong position due to Trainium, real P&L efficiencies from robotics in retail over the next 18 months, and underappreciated Nova models
summary_extended: Baker argues that Amazon is in a stronger competitive position than the market appreciates across three distinct vectors: its proprietary Trainium AI chip, robotics-driven retail efficiency, and undervalued Nova foundation models. On the chip side, Baker highlights Trainium 3's technical architecture — specifically its switch scale-up network — as enabling economical Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model inference, positioning Amazon to reduce dependence on NVIDIA and lower AI infrastructure costs. Baker expects robotics deployments in Amazon's retail and fulfillment operations to produce visible, measurable P&L improvements within 18 months, a catalyst he believes is underappreciated by the market. He also contends that Amazon's Nova models are better than their public reputation suggests, though he stops short of claiming they rival top-tier frontier models. The combined implication is that AMZN is a buy with multiple underappreciated near-to-medium-term earnings catalysts.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: Coding is the fastest path to AGI/ASI and will continue to be the dominant high-value AI use case capturing frontier model revenue
summary_extended: Baker argues that coding is the dominant high-value AI use case and will remain so for the foreseeable future, citing Replit founder Amjad Masad's 'bitter lesson adjacent' insight that a model capable of coding can write code to do virtually anything, making coding the fastest path to AGI and ASI. The reasoning is structural: coding demand appears inexhaustible, enterprises will not tolerate second-tier code quality and therefore will not trade down from frontier models, and proprietary coding data accumulated by players like Cursor and xAI creates durable competitive moats. The investment implication is that frontier model providers capturing coding revenue — particularly those with coding-specific data advantages — are best positioned, and that coding will continue to concentrate AI revenue at the frontier rather than commoditizing to cheaper models. Baker corroborates this view with Anthropic revenue data and Cursor's growth trajectory, expressing high conviction with no hedging on the thesis.

theme: Open source AI bullish for hardware
source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: Open-source AI success is bullish for compute/hardware providers, not bearish for the AI ecosystem
summary_extended: Baker argues that the rise of open-source AI models is structurally bullish for compute and hardware providers, not bearish for the AI ecosystem as commonly feared. His reasoning is that if frontier proprietary models face margin compression from open-source competition, AI spending does not disappear — it shifts toward compute infrastructure, since open-source models still require massive hardware to train and run at scale. The better open-source models perform, the more inference and training compute is demanded across a broader set of users and enterprises who would otherwise have paid for proprietary API access. This reframes the conventional open-source bear case (that it destroys AI monetization) into a compute bull case: commoditization of model intelligence accelerates hardware consumption. The investment implication is to be long compute and semiconductor infrastructure providers that supply the GPUs, networking, and accelerators powering this expanded workload.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: SpaceX IPO post-listing drawdown risk is real but the lock-up and employee liquidity history mitigate selling pressure
summary_extended: Baker argues that while historical data on major tech IPOs (Facebook, Twitter, Alibaba, Shopify) shows average maximum post-IPO drawdowns exceeding 50%, SpaceX's situation is structurally different in ways that may meaningfully reduce post-listing selling pressure. His reasoning centers on three mitigating factors: SpaceX employees have had liquidity events every six months for approximately ten years (~20 opportunities to sell), Elon Musk owns roughly 50% of the company and will be locked up for 365 days post-IPO, and the stock is expected to enter major indices quickly, creating sustained institutional buying demand. Baker acknowledges this is 'utterly unprecedented' territory and explicitly states he does not know what will happen in the short term, framing the honest investor posture as uncertainty. His practical recommendation is a 'set-it-and-forget-it' approach with tactical position sizing around volatility rather than attempting to time the post-IPO drawdown.
